does: This paper introduces Soohak, a 439-problem benchmark of research-level mathematics problems authored from scratch by 64 professional mathematicians to evaluate frontier large language models. The benchmark comprises two subsets, with the Challenge subset showing that state-of-the-art closed-weight models like Gemini-3-Pro, GPT-5, and Claude-Opus-4.5 achieve only 30.4%, 26.4%, and 10.4% respectively, while leading open-weight models remain below 15%. Unlike olympiad-style benchmarks that measure step-by-step reasoning, Soohak targets problems that require reasoning to advance mathematical knowledge itself, providing a substantially larger and more challenging evaluation set than existing research-level benchmarks such as Riemann Bench and FrontierMath-Tier 4.
connects: This work extends recent efforts to move beyond olympiad-style problem benchmarks, following landmark achievements like frontier LLMs attaining gold-medal IMO performance. It directly responds to the scarcity of research-level mathematical benchmarks identified in the community, building on prior attempts such as Riemann Bench and FrontierMath-Tier 4, which contained only 25 and 50 problems respectively. The benchmark represents a systematic scaling of the evaluation frontier, addressing the challenge of sourcing authentic research-level problems by engaging professional mathematicians rather than procedurally generating or repurposing existing academic problems.
enables: Soohak establishes a new evaluation frontier for assessing whether frontier models can perform mathematics research rather than merely solve pre-existing problems. By providing substantial headroom even for cutting-edge models, it enables comparative analysis of next-generation LLM capabilities and can drive development toward genuine mathematical discovery. The benchmark's design with mathematician-authored problems and multiple evaluation dimensions beyond standard problem-solving opens pathways for studying reasoning depth, conjecture formation, and proof validation in automated mathematical research. It provides a foundation for longitudinal tracking of LLM progress on genuinely novel mathematical challenges and may guide future architectural or training innovations targeting research-level reasoning.
